questions.question: In the recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), what does 'a' represent?
options: The total running time
The number of subproblems created at each recursive step
The size reduction factor
The cost of the non-recursive work
questions.explanation: 'a' is the number of subproblems. For merge sort, a=2 because each call splits the problem into 2 halves. This directly affects how the recursion tree branches at each level.

questions.question: Which of these recurrence relations describes binary search?
questions.explanation: Binary search discards half the input each step (a=1, b=2) and does constant comparison work (f(n)=1). This gives T(n) = T(n/2) + 1, which the Master Theorem resolves to Theta(log n).

questions.question: For T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n, compute n^(log_b(a)). Which Master Theorem case applies?
options: Case 1: n^(log_b(a)) = n, f(n) = n, so T(n) = Theta(n log n)
Case 1: n^(log_b(a)) = n^2, f(n) = n grows slower, so T(n) = Theta(n^2)
Case 2: n^(log_b(a)) = n, f(n) = n, so T(n) = Theta(n log n)
Case 3: f(n) = n grows faster, so T(n) = Theta(n)
questions.explanation: Here a=4, b=2, so n^(log_2 4) = n^2. Since f(n) = n = O(n^(2-1)), this is Case 1: the root dominates. The answer is Theta(n^2), not Theta(n log n).

questions.question: The Master Theorem fails when f(n) = n sin(n). Why?
options: Because sin(n) makes the recurrence non-recursive
Because n sin(n) oscillates and doesn't fit the polynomial comparison n^(log_b(a)) vs f(n)
Because the recurrence has too many subproblems
Because sin(n) is not a valid function for recurrences
questions.explanation: The Master Theorem requires comparing f(n) to n^(log_b(a)) in terms of polynomial growth rates. n sin(n) oscillates between -n and n, so it isn't consistently Omega(n^k) or O(n^k) for any k. You'd need to fall back to the recursion tree or substitution method.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of merge sort, expressed as a recurrence relation?
questions.explanation: Merge sort divides the array into two halves (2T(n/2)), then merges them in linear time (+n). This gives T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, which the Master Theorem resolves to Theta(n log n).
